DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-06-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
T. Scott Tate, Esq. of The Miller Law Firm, 415.437.1800, appearing for the Plaintiff;
Eileen R. Ridley, Esq. of Foley & Lardner LLP, 415.434.4484, appearing for the Defendants;
Nicholas P. Honkamp, Esq. of Foley & Lardner LLP, 415.984.9885, appearing for the Defendants.
DEFENDANTS RCDC 942 MARKET STREET, LLC AND RCDC 942 L.L.C.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter called, ARGUMENT held.

Ruling: At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as modified: Defendants RCDC 942 Market Street, LLC and RCDC 942 L.L.C.'s demurrer to the first through fourth and sixth through eighth causes of action in plaintiff 942 Market Street Owners' Association's complaint is: a) sustained with THIRTY days leave to amend as to first through third, sixth and seventh causes of action for violation of residential building standards, negligence, strict liability, breach of fiduciary duty and violation of governing documents and b) sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth and eighth causes of action for breach of implied warranty of habitability and unlawful LLC distributions. Because there are five related builder defendants, each with what appears to be a different role regarding the real property that is the subject of this case, as to each cause of action 942 Market Street Owners' Association should allege why it contends each of these demurring defendants is liable instead of lumping the builder defendants all together in a collectively defined term. The Association should add facts explaining how the demurring defendants are liable since they were not in existence when the project was constructed and are not parties to the CC&Rs. Defendants' remaining arguments on the first cause of action lack merit. Civil Code 930(b), which discusses staying an action, indicates that the failure to comply with the notice/repair requirement is not fatal to a claim. There is no time bar as the complaint was filed within the ten-year statute of repose. On the second and third causes of action, the Association is granted leave to allege facts showing damage to the commercial units and that it has the right to maintain claims on behalf of the entities occupying the commercial units. Defendants correctly cite McMillin Albany, LLC v. Super. Crt. (2018) 4 Cal.5th 241 for the proposition that Civil Code 896 and 897 preempt residential common law claims. Fairly read, the complaint is asserting residential claims. In addition, the CC&Rs indicate that those are the only claims that the Association can bring. (CC&Rs 1.3, 1.7, 1.10, 1.11, 1.12, 1.22, 1.26, 1.34, 2.2.) The implied warranty of merchantability claim fails because a condominium or commercial unit is not a "consumer good" that is within the scope of the Song-Beverly Act per Atkinson v Elk Corp. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 739, 757. The unlawful distributions claim fails because the demurring defendants are Delaware entities. The order was signed as modified. (see order for full details)
